title: L2: Costumes & Movement
description: Build a walking, animating character using two threads — movement stays smooth while animation runs on its own timer.

steps.title: What are we building?
steps.content:
Costumes & Movement 🎮

In this lesson you will build a character that moves with the arrow keys and animates through its costumes — both happening at the same time.

The key idea is threads. In PyScratch, each thread is an independent block of code that runs in parallel. A wait() in the animation thread never slows down movement because the two threads do not share a timer.

Thread 1 — movement
def game_start():
    set_rotation_style("left-right")
    while True:
        if key_pressed("right"):
            change_x(5)
            point_in_direction(90)
        if key_pressed("left"):
            change_x(-5)
            point_in_direction(-90)
        if_on_edge_bounce()
Thread 2 — animation
def game_start():
    while True:
        next_costume()
        wait(0.1)

⚠️ Make sure your sprite has at least two costumes before running the code. Click the Costumes tab in TurboWarp to check.

steps.title: Why Use Two Threads?
steps.content:
Why use two threads? 🤔

Click through the example below to see the problem with putting movement and animation in a single loop, and exactly why separate threads fix it.

steps.title: Build Your Game
steps.content:
Build it yourself! 🛠️

Follow the guided steps below. The arrow keys on your keyboard move between steps — or use the buttons.

💡 Make sure your sprite has at least two costumes first — switch to the Costumes tab in TurboWarp to check.
steps.js:
PyScratchLesson.createStepper({containerId:'ps-m-build',height:540,steps:[{title:'Step 1 — The Threads panel',text:'The Threads panel on the left side of PyScratch lists all your code blocks. Right now there is one default thread. You are going to rename it and add a second one.',interactive:false,highlight:'threads',highlightLabel:'Threads panel'},{title:'Step 2 — Set up your two threads',text:'Rename the existing thread to movement by clicking the pencil ✏️ icon next to it. Then click the + button to add a second thread and name it animation. You should see both listed in the panel.',interactive:true,highlight:'threads',highlightLabel:'Rename & click + here'},{title:'Step 3 — The movement code (read first)',text:'Click your movement thread to select it. Here is the code you will type in the next step:

def game_start():
    set_rotation_style("left-right")
    while True:
        if key_pressed("right"):
            change_x(5)
            point_in_direction(90)
        if key_pressed("left"):
            change_x(-5)
            point_in_direction(-90)
        if_on_edge_bounce()

No wait() here — this thread runs as fast as the screen refreshes.',interactive:false,highlight:'editor',highlightLabel:'You will type here'},{title:'Step 4 — Type the movement code',text:'Select your movement thread and type the code from the previous step.

💡 Tips: press Tab to indent, press Enter after a colon and PyScratch will auto-indent. Take your time — indentation matters in Python.',interactive:true,highlight:'threads'},{title:'Step 5 — The animation code (read first)',text:'Now look at the animation thread. Here is its code:

def game_start():
    while True:
        next_costume()
        wait(0.1)

The wait(0.1) makes costumes cycle 10 times per second. It only pauses this thread — movement is completely unaffected.',interactive:false,highlight:'threads',highlightLabel:'Click animation thread'},{title:'Step 6 — Type the animation code',text:'Click your animation thread to select it, then type the animation code from the previous step.

Keep the wait(0.1) — without it the costume would flicker too fast to see.',interactive:true,highlight:'editor'},{title:'Step 7 — Run your game!',text:'Both threads are written. Click the green flag to start your game.

Use the left and right arrow keys to move the sprite. Watch how it animates smoothly as it moves — that is two threads running in parallel. 🎉',interactive:true,highlight:'green-flag',highlightLabel:'Click to run!'},{title:'Challenge 1 — Add up and down movement',text:'Add vertical movement to your movement thread. Inside the while True: loop, after the left/right checks, add:

if key_pressed("up"):
    change_y(5)
if key_pressed("down"):
    change_y(-5)

You do not need to change the animation thread at all.',interactive:true,highlight:'threads'},{title:'Challenge 2 — Adjust the animation speed',text:'Switch to your animation thread and experiment with the wait() value:

🐇 wait(0.05) — faster animation (20fps)
🐢 wait(0.25) — slower animation (4fps)

Which speed looks best with your sprite? When you are happy, click Finish ✓.',interactive:true,highlight:'editor'}]});

quizQuestions.q: Why does putting animation and movement in the same loop cause problems?
options: The wait() slows the whole loop — including key checks — to 10 times per second
PyScratch can only define one game_start() function per sprite
The while True loop uses too much memory when there is a wait() inside
next_costume() and change_x() cannot be called in the same function

quizQuestions.q: If the animation thread calls wait(0.1), what happens to the movement thread?
options: The movement thread carries on running — wait() only pauses the thread it is called in
The movement thread also waits 0.1 seconds before the next key check
The movement thread stops permanently until the animation finishes
Both threads restart from the beginning of game_start()

quizQuestions.q: What does wait(0.1) in the animation thread control?
options: How often the costume changes — 10 times per second
How fast the sprite moves across the stage
How long the green flag takes to start the game
The maximum frame rate of the entire game

quizQuestions.q: What does set_rotation_style("left-right") do in the movement thread?
options: Makes the sprite flip horizontally when moving left or right, instead of rotating
Locks the sprite so it can only move left and right
Rotates the sprite 90 degrees when a key is pressed
Stops the sprite bouncing off the top and bottom edges

quizQuestions.q: Which of these is the correct code for the animation thread?
options: def game_start():\n    next_costume()\n    wait(0.1)
while True:\n    next_costume()\n    wait(0.1)
def animation():\n    while True:\n        next_costume()
